text:
what is wrong with this pg query?
SET statement_timeout = '30min'; SELECT COUNT(*) FROM device_state.device WHERE is_active = true AND tenant_uuid = ANY(ARRAY['cd17ecbe-0713-41b4-99e4-c4ee05015c52']);
SET
ERROR:  operator does not exist: uuid = text
LINE 1: ...ate.device WHERE is_active = true AND tenant_uuid = ANY(ARRA...
                                                             ^
HINT:  No operator matches the given name and argument types. You might need to add explicit type casts.

The error occurs because you're trying to compare a UUID column (tenant_uuid) with text values in the array. You need to explicitly cast the text values to UUID type in the array. Here's the corrected query:

```sql
SET statement_timeout = '30min';
SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM device_state.device 
WHERE is_active = true 
AND tenant_uuid = ANY(ARRAY['cd17ecbe-0713-41b4-99e4-c4ee05015c52']::uuid[]);
```

The key fix is adding `::uuid[]` to cast the text array to an array of UUIDs. This ensures that the comparison is between values of the same type (UUID = UUID) rather than (UUID = text).
what is that Array function, can it take multiple comma separeted uuidsc/
Yes, the ARRAY constructor can take multiple comma-separated values. Here's an example with multiple UUIDs:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM device_state.device 
WHERE is_active = true 
AND tenant_uuid = ANY(ARRAY['cd17ecbe-0713-41b4-99e4-c4ee05015c52', 
                           'another-uuid-here', 
                           'third-uuid-here']::uuid[]);
```

The ARRAY constructor is basically creating an array of values that you can use with the ANY operator. It's equivalent to writing:
"where tenant_uuid matches any of these values in the array"

You could also write this using IN which is often more readable:
```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM device_state.device 
WHERE is_active = true 
AND tenant_uuid IN ('cd17ecbe-0713-41b4-99e4-c4ee05015c52'::uuid, 
                   'another-uuid-here'::uuid, 
                   'third-uuid-here'::uuid);
```

Both approaches will work the same way.
summary: UUID Type Casting in Postgres Query
